notes: Hierarchical enterprise directory. Introduced NetWare 4 (1993). Ported to NT/Unix 1997-1998. Rebranded eDirectory ~2000. Acquired chain led to OpenText ownership.
Microsoft directory service for Windows 2000. Aberdeen predicted release mid-1998; actually shipped February 2000. Became dominant enterprise directory.
Standards-based directory access protocol. LDAPv3 approved December 1997 as proposed Internet standard. Now universal directory protocol.
OSI-based directory standard. Basis for NDS and AD conceptual models. Largely superseded by LDAP for practical deployments.
NetWare NOS with Internet/intranet capabilities. Novell's core product. Succeeded by NetWare 5/6 and eventually Open Enterprise Server on SUSE Linux.
Dominant enterprise NOS through mid-1990s. Lost market share to Windows NT and Windows 2000. Formally discontinued 2009.
Email and groupware suite built on NDS. Competed with Lotus Notes and Microsoft Exchange. Still marketed by OpenText.
LAN management product integrated with NDS. Integration with CA Unicenter TNG announced. Discontinued early 2000s.
CA's enterprise management suite. Integrated with ManageWise for WAN coverage. Evolved into CA Technologies management portfolio.
Directory integration with VPN identified as key infrastructure need. VPN technology became standard enterprise connectivity.
NDS-based application deployment tool allowing remote software push to workstations. Predecessor to modern endpoint management.
Banyan's enterprise directory service. Competitor to NDS. Declined with Banyan's enterprise business.
